title: Calculating ΔS° & Standard Entropy
subtitle: From a table of S° values to ΔS° for a reaction
subject: Chemistry
syllabusDotPoints: Calculate the standard entropy change (ΔS°) of a reaction using standard entropies of products and reactants
lessonIntent: Students can calculate ΔS° for a reaction from a table of standard entropies and verify the sign matches qualitative prediction.
examSkill: Apply ΔS° = Σ S°(products) − Σ S°(reactants) with coefficients, in units J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹, and confirm the sign agrees with Δn(gas).
productionNotes: Mirrors site lesson `subjects/chemistry/year11/module4/lesson12.html`. Worked example: Haber N2+3H2→2NH3 (verbatim from site). S°[N2]=191.6, S°[H2]=130.7, S°[NH3]=192.4 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹. ΔS° = 2(192.4) − [191.6 + 3(130.7)] = 384.8 − 583.7 = −198.9 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹. Negative because Δn(gas) = 2 − 4 = −2. Misconception: 'S° of an element is zero' (unlike ΔH°f, S° of elements is NOT zero, they have real entropy).

scenes.caption: Calculating ΔS° and Standard Entropy. Year 11 Chemistry, Module 4.

voiceover.text: Calculating delta S and Standard Entropy. Year eleven Chemistry, Module four.

scenes.heading: How do we put a real number on disorder?
scenes.body: Last lesson you predicted the sign of ΔS by counting gas moles. That gives a yes-or-no. To actually use entropy in calculations, especially for Gibbs free energy next lesson, you need a real number, in J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹.

bullets.text: Qualitative ΔS prediction: counts gas moles, gives a sign.
Quantitative ΔS°: comes from a table of S° values.
Units: joules per kelvin per mole, not kilojoules.
The sign must match your qualitative prediction.

scenes.callout: Quantitative ΔS° puts a real number on what L11 predicted.
scenes.caption: ΔS° gives an exact value, in J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹.

voiceover.text: Last lesson you predicted the sign of delta S by counting gas moles. That gives a yes-or-no. But to actually use entropy in calculations — especially for Gibbs free energy next lesson — you need a real number, in joules per kelvin per mole. Think of qualitative versus quantitative entropy like saying a building is tall versus saying it's two hundred and fifty metres. But unlike height, where you can just measure, we can't measure absolute entropy directly — so we look up standard values in a table. This lesson is how to use that table.

scenes.heading: ΔS° = Σ S°(products) − Σ S°(reactants)
scenes.body: Same shape as the formation-enthalpy formula from Lesson 7. Sum the products, sum the reactants, subtract, with coefficients.

bullets.text: Scale each S° value by its stoichiometric coefficient.
Units: J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ (notice: joules, not kilojoules).
Crucially: S° of elements is NOT zero (unlike ΔH°f).

scenes.caption: Sum products, sum reactants, subtract; all elements have S° > 0.

voiceover.text: The formula has the same shape as the formation-enthalpy one from Lesson seven. Delta S zero equals the sum of standard entropies of products minus the sum for reactants — each scaled by its coefficient. Units are joules per kelvin per mole — and the joules matters; they're not kilojoules, which catches people out next lesson when we mix delta S with delta H. And here's the critical difference from delta H of formation: standard entropy of an element is not zero. Every substance has real positive entropy, including elements.

scenes.heading: ΔS° for the Haber process
scenes.question: Calculate ΔS° for N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) → 2NH₃(g). Use: S°[N₂(g)] = 191.6, S°[H₂(g)] = 130.7, S°[NH₃(g)] = 192.4 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹.
scenes.coachNote: Scale by coefficients. Sum products, sum reactants, subtract. Then check the sign against Δn(gas).
steps: Formula: ΔS° = Σ S°(products) − Σ S°(reactants).
Δn(gas) check: 2 − 4 = −2, so ΔS° should be negative, confirmed.
Answer: ΔS° = 198.9 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ decrease (i.e. −198.9 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹).
scenes.caption: ΔS°(Haber) = 384.8 − 583.7 = −198.9 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹.

voiceover.text: Let's do the Haber process. Sum the products — two times one-ninety-two-point-four for ammonia — three hundred and eighty-four-point-eight. Sum the reactants — one times one-ninety-one-point-six for nitrogen, plus three times one-thirty-point-seven for hydrogen — total five hundred and eighty-three-point-seven. Delta S zero is products minus reactants — three-eighty-four-point-eight minus five-eighty-three-point-seven — minus one hundred and ninety-eight-point-nine joules per kelvin per mole. Quick sanity check: four moles of gas become two, so delta n of gas is minus two — and the negative sign matches. The qualitative prediction and the calculation agree.

scenes.heading: "S° of an element is zero"
scenes.body: Students often think: "ΔH°f of elements is zero, so S° must be too." That's the wrong instinct, it confuses two different reference conventions. The correct rule: standard entropy of any substance, including elements, is NOT zero. Every substance has real positive entropy because it has many possible microstates. So S°[N₂] = 191.6, S°[H₂] = 130.7, both included in the reactant sum.
scenes.callout: ΔH°f of elements = 0. But S° of elements ≠ 0. Different conventions.
scenes.caption: Unlike ΔH°f, S° of an element is NOT zero, include it.

voiceover.text: Here's the trap. Students learn that delta H of formation of elements is zero, and assume standard entropy of elements is too. Wrong instinct — it confuses two different reference conventions. The correct rule: standard entropy of any substance, including elements, is not zero. Every substance has real positive entropy because it has many possible microstates available to it. So S of N two is one-ninety-one-point-six, S of H two is one-thirty-point-seven — both go into the reactant sum, in full. Drop them and your answer is off by hundreds of joules per kelvin per mole.

scenes.heading: Your turn
scenes.question: Calculate ΔS° for 2H₂(g) + O₂(g) → 2H₂O(l). Use S°[H₂(g)] = 130.7, S°[O₂(g)] = 205.2, S°[H₂O(l)] = 69.9 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹.
scenes.pausePrompt: Pause: sum products (scaled), sum reactants (scaled), subtract.
answerSteps: Formula: ΔS° = Σ S°(products) − Σ S°(reactants).
Answer: ΔS° = 326.8 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ decrease (i.e. −326.8 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹), 3 mol gas → 0 mol gas + condensation.
scenes.caption: ΔS° = 139.8 − 466.6 = −326.8 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ (gas → liquid).

voiceover.text: Your turn. Calculate delta S zero for the combustion of hydrogen to liquid water. Sum the products: two times sixty-nine-point-nine for liquid water — one hundred and thirty-nine-point-eight. Sum the reactants: two times one-thirty-point-seven for H two, plus one times two-zero-five-point-two for O two — four hundred and sixty-six-point-six. Delta S zero is one-thirty-nine-point-eight minus four-sixty-six-point-six — minus three hundred and twenty-six-point-eight joules per kelvin per mole. Strongly negative, as expected: three moles of gas become two moles of liquid — a big collapse in disorder.

scenes.heading: Summary
scenes.finalPrompt: Always check: does the calculated sign match the gas-moles prediction?
points: ΔS° = Σ S°(products) − Σ S°(reactants), scaled by coefficients.
Units are J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹, joules, not kilojoules.
S° of an element is NOT zero, include it (unlike ΔH°f).
Sanity-check the sign against Δn(gas): more gas → ΔS° positive.
ΔS° in J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹ feeds straight into Gibbs free energy next lesson.
scenes.caption: Sum products, subtract reactants, sanity-check the sign.

voiceover.text: Five takeaways. One: delta S zero equals the sum of standard entropies of products minus reactants, each scaled by its coefficient. Two: units are joules per kelvin per mole — joules, not kilojoules. Three: standard entropy of an element is not zero, unlike delta H of formation — include it. Four: sanity-check your sign against delta n of gas — more gas on the product side means delta S positive. Five: this calculated delta S zero feeds straight into Gibbs free energy next lesson. Decision rule: always check that your calculated sign matches the gas-moles prediction.
